coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots prepared by solid state method via citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline for selective and sensing detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Iqbal, Anam
Tian, Yuejun
Wang, Xudong
Gong, Deyan
Guo, Yali
Iqbal, Kanwal
Wang, Zhiping
Liu, Weisheng
Qin, Wenwu
coredata.dc:description: A new kind of convenient, low-cost, environmentally friendly, and simple one step synthetic route for the preparation of C-dots-based CDs (CA+Phen) fluorescent turn-off probe for Fe2+ and Fe3+ from 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen) using anhydrous citric acid (CA) as the sole precursor without any solvents by solid state method. Only the Fe2+ and Fe3+ can readily quench the fluorescence of the CDs (CA+Phen), indicating a remarkably high selectivity towards Fe2+ and Fe3+ over other metals with a detection limit as low as 20nM and 35nM. The CDs (CA+Phen) exhibited a low cytotoxicity and could label the HK-2 cells. Additionally, this CDs (CA+Phen) has been successfully applied for the determination of Fe in real sample of milk. Endowed with relatively high sensitivity and selectivity, the present sensor holds the potential to be applied for the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous media.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescent detection
Fe2+
Fe3+
Solid state method
Cell imaging

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: UV–vis absorption and steady-State and time-Resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
Cell culture, fluorescence imaging and cytotoxicity assay
3 spectroscopic properties of CDs (CA+Phen) with Fe2+ and Fe3+
Application of CDs (CA+Phen) in milk and cell (HK-2)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A–C) TEM images of CDs (CA+Phen) with different magnifications. (D) Size distribution histogram of CDs (CA+Phen).
The FTIR spectrum of (a) Citric acid CDs (CA) (b) 1–10 Phen CDs (Phen) and (c) CDs (CA+Phen).
Fluorescence emission spectra (excitation wavelength (λex) from 300 to 480nm) of CDs (CA+Phen) (150μg/mL) in aqueous solution.

Fluorescence spectra of CDs (CA+Phen) (150μg/mL) in the presence of different amounts of (a) Fe2+ and (b) Fe3+ in HEPES buffer at pH 7.4. Fe2+concentration from top to bottom: (0, 0.8, 1.2, 1.6, 2, 2.4, 2.8, 3.2, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 40, 48, 56, 64, 72, 80 and 100μM) (λex =360nm). Fe3+ concentration from top to bottom: (0, 0.8,1.6, 2.4, 3.2, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20, 24, 28, 32, 36, 40, 48, 56, 64, 72, 80, 100, 120, 140, 160, 180 and 200μM) (λex =360nm).
Calibration curves as a function of concentration of (a) Fe2+ (0–32μM) and (b) Fe3+ (0–50μM) in HEPES buffer at pH 7.4.
Fluorescence response of CDs (CA+Phen) (150μg/mL) in the presence of different metals (HEPES buffer at pH 7.4), (λex =360nm). All the concentration of the metal ions is 0.01M.
Schematic representation of formation of CDs (CA+Phen), the binding of CDs (CA+Phen) with Fe2+ and Fe3+and the quenching off CDs (CA+Phen) by Fe2+ and Fe3+.

Confocal fluorescence images of live HK-2 cells. (1) 1a-Bright field microscopy image of pure HK-2 cells, 1b- Fluorescence image of pure HK-2 cells, excited by ultraviolet light. (2) Fluorescence image of HK-2 cells incubated with CDs (CA+Phen) for 1h at 37°C. (3) Fluorescence image of CDs (CA+Phen)-stained HK-2 cells exposed to 80μM Fe2+ for 30min. (4) Fluorescence image of CDs (CA+Phen)-stained HK-2 cells exposed to 160μM Fe3+ for 30min.
Comparison of fluorescence CDs probes for Fe2+/Fe3+ based fluorescence quencher.
Determination of Fe in Real milk samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ICP-AES=inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: The average value of the five time measured samples.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots prepared by solid state method via citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline for selective and sensing detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province and State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry, College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Nonferrous Metal Chemistry and Resources Utilization of Gansu Province and State Key Laboratory of Applied Organic Chemistry
College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Urology, The Second Hospital of Lanzhou University, Lanzhou, Gansu Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Second Hospital of Lanzhou University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CDs (CA+Phen) could be potentially utilized as an excellent optical sensor for the quantitative analysis of Fe2+ or Fe3+ in living cells.

A new kind of convenient, low-cost, environmentally friendly, and simple one step synthetic route for the preparation of C-dots-based CDs (CA+Phen) fluorescent turn-off probe for Fe2+ and Fe3+ from 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen) using anhydrous citric acid (CA) as the sole precursor without any solvents by solid state method. Only the Fe2+ and Fe3+ can readily quench the fluorescence of the CDs (CA+Phen), indicating a remarkably high selectivity towards Fe2+ and Fe3+ over other metals with a detection limit as low as 20nM and 35nM. The CDs (CA+Phen) exhibited a low cytotoxicity and could label the HK-2 cells. Additionally, this CDs (CA+Phen) has been successfully applied for the determination of Fe in real sample of milk. Endowed with relatively high sensitivity and selectivity, the present sensor holds the potential to be applied for the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous media.

ce:section.ce:para: Over recent decade, there has been considerable interest for the detection of heavy and transition metal ions using fluorescent chemosensors because fluorometric technique is relatively sensitive. So an Important application of surface functionalization is oriented towards sensing [1–6], detection of heavy metals [1–7] or transition metal ion e.g. among these metals ions the iron i.e. Iron(II) and Iron(III) plays an important role in many chemical and biological process in living organism like the transport of oxygen, storage metabolism, electron transfer in enzymatic reaction and in mitochondrial respiratory chain [8–10] however its excessive accumulation in human body causes serious diseases like liver and kidney damage [11] due to the possibility of generating radicals. Therefore an accurate and fast determination of Fe is an important issue both in the environment and in biological system.

Currently the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ can be achieved by using different analytical methods like atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) [12], flame atomic absorption spectroscopy, mass spectroscopy [13], coupled plasma spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [14], electrochemical [15], spectrophotometric [16], colorimetric [17,18], electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) [16]. But the major drawback of these techniques is as lengthy pretreatment sample procedures, and also to some extent the sophisticated instrumentation. Fluorescence offers considerable advantages over other methods for Fe2+ and Fe3+ measurements inside live cells because of its generally non-destructive character, high sensitivity, selectivity and fast detection. Most the fluorescence chemosensors or probes are mainly based on two concepts: (1) Fe2+ and Fe3+ triggered turn-on fluorescence of fluorophores (so called chemodosimeters) [19–21] and (2) fluorescence quenching of fluorophores due to the paramagnetic nature of Fe [22]. There is still a great need for the development of Fe2+ and Fe3+ probes with improved properties, such as solubility in aqueous media, no (photo) toxicity to live cells, high fluorescence quantum yields, and high sensitivity and high selectivity for Fe detection.

Recently, fluorescent nano particles have attracted great interest in developing fluorescent probes owing to their unique and fascinating optical properties. Carbon dots have received much attention among fluorescent nanoparticles in striking contrast to organic dyes and quantum dots. Carbon nano dots (CDs) are the most popular carbon- based nanomaterials in recent years and have successfully overcome some defects of traditional semiconductor quantum dots [23–28]. So Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have recently drawn immense attention owing to their unique properties such as high water solubility, good optical properties along with chemical inertness, excellent cell permeability, superiority in photo stability, fluorescence non-blinking emission, excitation-dependent emission tunability, flexibility in surface functionalization, easiness of preparation, remarkably low cytotoxicity, environmentally friendliness and excellent biocompatibility [29–31].

Like QDs the C-dots can also be employed to fabricate fluorescent probes and sensors. Recently through surface functionalization, CDs have been endowed with molecular recognition ability and applied in chemical sensing. For example Chen JR and his co-workers recently reported a carbon dot for sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of Hg2+ and Fe3+ ions [32]. The fluorescence of C-dots could be significantly quenched by Hg2+ and Fe3+. Wang et al. reported a fluorescence quenchometric (turn-off) method for determination of ferric ion using boron-doped carbon dots [33]. Qu et al. reported a fluorescent sensing platform for the label-free detection of Fe3+ and dopamine. The fluorescence of C-dots could be significantly quenched by Fe3+ion [34]. Dhenadhayalan and Lin reported a chemically induced fluorescence switching of carbon-dots and its multiple logic gate implementation, based fluorescence off-on (Fe3+-S2O3 2−) and on-off (Zn2+-PO4 3−) sensors for the detection of metal ions and anions [35].

1,10-Phenanthroline is an ideal candidate for application in owing to its structural features such as 2 N-atom placed in juxta position created binding sites for metal cations. Thus 1,10-phenanthroline can determine Fe2+ in high sensitivity and selectivity by UV spectra, but not any fluorescence since 1,10-phenanthroline doesn’t have fluorescent properties. In this contribution, we develop a new kind of convenient and simple one step synthetic route for the preparation of C-dots-based CDs (CA+Phen) fluorescent turn-off probe for Fe2+ and Fe3+ from 1,10-phenanthroline (Phen) using anhydrous citric acid (CA) without any solvents by solid state method as the sole precursor. 1,10-phenanthroline can functionalize the surface of anhydrous citric acid which serve as a carbon source. The FL interactions between the CDs (CA+Phen) and Fe2+ and Fe3+ were investigated in detail. Moreover the quenching mechanism is explored.

Anhydrous citric acid was purchased from Guangfu Reagent Company (Tianjin, China). 1,10-Phenanthroline (99%) was purchased from Sinopharm chemical Regent Co. Ltd (Beijing, China). The chlorides of (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, and NH4 +) sulfate of Fe2+, acetate of Zn2+ and nitrates of Hg2+ and Ag+ were purchased from heowns (Tianjing, China). All reagents are of analytical grade. Ultrapure water obtained from a Milli-Q ultrapure (18.2MΩcm) system was used in all experiments.

XRD measurement was performed on X-ray diffractometer (Philips X’Pert Holland) with Cu Kα radiation (λ=1.54059), with opening voltage and current at 40kV and 35mA. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy at an accelerating voltage of 200Kev. Dynamic light scattered (DLS) was got on a BI-200SM (USA Brookhaven) at room temperature. The Fourier transform infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectra were measured by Nicolet 360 FTIR spectrometer with the KBr pellet technique. Elemental analysis for Fe was analyzed by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) (Thermo Jarrel Ash,Franklin, MA, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV–vis absorption and steady-State and time-Resolved fluorescence spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded on a Varian UV-Cary100 spectrophotometer, and for the corrected steady-state fluorescence emission spectra, a FLS920 spectrofluorometer was employed. Fluorescence quantum yields were determined by comparison of the integral of the emission bands with the one of quinine sulfate/0.5M H2SO4 (Φ =0.54) [36]. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured on an Edinburgh Instruments FLS920 equipped with the light emitting diodes (excitation wavelength 360nm), using the time-correlated single photon counting technique in 2048 channels at room temperature.

Histograms of the instrument response functions (using a LUDOX scatter) and sample decays were recorded until they typically reached 1.0×104 counts in the peak channel. Obtained histograms were fitted as sums of the exponentials, using Gaussian-weighted nonlinear least squares fitting based on Marquardt-Levenberg minimization implemented in the software package of the instrument. The fitting parameters (decay times and preexponential factors) were determined by minimizing the reduced chi-square χ2. An additional graphical method was used to judge the quality of the fit that included plots of surfaces (“carpets”) of the weighted residuals vs channel number. All curve fittings presented here had χ2 values<1.1.

Photoluminescent CDs (CA+Phen) were prepared by solid state method of anhydrous citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline. In a typical synthesis 0.90g of anhydrous citric acid and 0.10g 1,10-phenanthroline were transferred into a 50mL Teflon lined autoclave without any solvents by solid state method and heated at 180°C for 2hThe viscous yellowish- brown photoluminescent CDs (CA+Phen) was obtained. Finally, the CDs (CA+Phen) were dialyzed overnight using dialysis membranes of 1000 cut off, diluted to 100.00mL with water.
In order to make the comparison, individual citric acid CDs (CA) or individual 1,10-phenanthroline CDs (Phen) was also made.

The detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ was performed at room temperature in aqueous solution of CDs (CA+Phen) at pH 7.4 using HEPES (12.5mM) .The standard stock solution of Fe2+ and Fe3+ were prepared by dissolving the appropriate amount of FeSO4·7H2O and FeCl3.6H2O. To evaluate the sensitivity towards Fe2+/Fe3+ different concentration of 0.01M Fe2+/Fe3+ (pH 7.4) were added into the aqueous solution of CDs (CA+Phen) and the mixed solution were sonicated for 2min before spectral measurements. The PL spectra were operated by operating the fluorescence spectrometer with an excitation wavelength of 360nm. The similar procedure was performed for the detection of other metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture, fluorescence imaging and cytotoxicity assay
ce:section.ce:para: Human kidney-2 cells (HK-2) were obtained from the cell bank of shanghai science Academy. The cells were seeded in culture dishes containing DMEM medium with 10%, calf serum, 100unitsmL−1 penicillin, 100μgmL−1streptomycin and 100μgmL−1 neomycin, incubated in a fully humidified incubator at 37°C with 5% CO2 .when the cells reached 80% confluence with normal morphology, these cells were ready for further experiments.

After removal of the culture medium, cells were incubated with CDs (CA+Phen) in 1.0mL of fresh culture medium for 2h. Before imaging measurement, the cells were washed three times with PBS to remove the residual CDs (CA+Phen). To observe fluorescence changes of the medium, 100μM FeSO4·7H2O or FeCl3·6H2O was added into the CDs (CA+Phen)-loaded cells and then further incubated for 0.5h at 37°C. The change of fluorescence was observed using a confocal microscope and the excitation wavelength of confocal microscope was 360nm. Cells were observed under an Olympus FV1000-IX81 laser confocal microscope.

The commercial kit (CCK-8 Kit) has been employed for the cytotoxicity assay. HK-2Cells were seeded in 96-well assay plates at a density of 104 cells per well (100μL total volume/well) for 24h. The as-prepared CDs (CA+Phen), (0μgmL−1, 20μgmL−1, 40μgmL−1, 80μgmL−1,160μgmL−1 and 200μgmL−1) were added in the serum-free medium and incubated with the cells for 24h after that. The cells were cultivated in serum-free medium with 10% CCK-8 solution for 2h after washed with PBS for three times. The optical absorbance of the cells was detected at 450nm through a microplate reader (German Berthold Mithras 2LB943). The control experiment was finished by detecting the growth culture medium without CDs (CA+Phen).

The CDs (CA+Phen) in this synthesis were prepared by the one step solid state synthesis of anhydrous citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline at 180°C leads to a yellowish brown dispersion. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) results suggest that the CDs (CA+Phen) are mostly spherical dots that are well separated from each other with a size less than 6nm. The HRTEM image of CDs (CA+Phen) shows the high crystallinity with the lattice spacing of 0.215nm and 0.204nm, which is close to the (002) facet of graphite. The particle size distribution is obtained from dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements, and the result shows that the average hydrodynamic diameter of the CDs (CA+Phen) is 5.7nm (Fig. 1 D).
The XRD patterns of the CDs (CA+Phen) displayed a broad peak centered at 2θ=20° which is attributed to highly disordered carbon atoms as shown in Fig. 2
To investigate the composition and functional group of CDs (CA+Phen), FTIR analysis was performed. Fig. 3 presents the FTIR spectrum of CDs (CA+Phen).

With respect to pure CDs (CA), the peaks at about 3375cm−1 can be ascribed to the characteristic absorption bands of the OH stretching vibration mode. The peak at 1396cm−1 can be attributed to the symmetric stretching vibrations of carboxylate (COO−) [37,38]. The characteristic absorption band of CH stretching at 3070cm−1 is also observed, and the bands in the range 1700cm−1 implied the existence of CO groups. The band in the range 1207cm−1 implied the existence of COC group, while with respect to pure CDs (Phen) the peak at about 3405cm−1 are assigned to (NH) and (OH) vibrational stretching modes. The absorption band of CH stretching at 3113cm−1 is also observed, the bands in the range 1587 and 1417 are CC and CN respectively.

With respect to CDs (CA+Phen), the peak at 3100–3500cm−1 are assigned to (OH) and (NH) stretching vibration of carboxylic acid and amine groups respectively. These functional groups improve the hydrophilicity and stability of CDs (CA+Phen) in aqueous system. Absorption bands from 3050 to 3150cm−1 are assigned to (CH) of aromatic stretching of CDs (CA+Phen). The absorption band at 2560cm−1 are assigned to (NCO) stretching vibration. The absorption band at 1600–1700cm−1 are assigned to (CO) stretching vibration from (COOH) group. The peak at 1715cm−1 is primarily due to the amide linkage (CONH) verifies the attachment of some residue of 1,10-phenanthroline on the surface of CDs. Absorption band at 1370–1465cm−1 are assigned to (CH) bending vibration. The absorption band at 1197cm−1 are assigned to COC respectively due to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibration. The absorption band at 848cm−1 and 1404cm−1 are assigned to benzene (residue of 1,10-phenanthroline).

UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (CA+Phen) dissolved in water is depicted in Fig. S1. The absorption spectra are of similar shape as those of described CDs [39,40]. CDs (CA+Phen) show broad absorption bands with absorption maxima at 271nm which attributable to the π-π* transition and another at around 310nm.

It has been reported that the fluorescence emission spectra and photoluminescent intensity of CDs (CA+Phen) depend on the excitation wavelength. To further investigate the optical properties of the CDs (CA+Phen) a series of emission spectra for the CDs (CA+Phen) were recorded at different excitation wavelength ranging from 300 to 480nm, the result shown in Fig. 4 . The maximum excitation wavelength of CDs (CA+Phen) is observed at 360nm. The emission spectrum shows a strong peak at 440nm when excited at 360nm. The blue fluorescence of CDs (CA+Phen) in aqueous solution observed at 365nm UV-light can be seen by naked eye. As shown in Fig. 4. When the excited wavelength changed from 300 to 480nm, the emission peak shows red shift. It shows that the CDs (CA+Phen) exhibit an excitation dependent luminescent behavior, which is similar to other luminescent carbon nanoparticles. The CDs (CA+Phen) is very stable because its fluorescence is almost unchanged after two month storage. This excellent stability makes the CDs (CA+Phen) as fluorescent probe. Using quinine sulfate (quantum yield=54%) as a standard, the quantum yield of the present CDs (CA+Phen) was determined to be 10% at an excitation wave length of 360nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: 3 spectroscopic properties of CDs (CA+Phen) with Fe2+ and Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: Complex formation was investigated for CDs (CA+Phen) with alkali, alkaline-earth, and transition metal ions in HEPES buffer. Upon addition of Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, NH4 +, Zn2+, Hg2+ and Ag+ in the μM concentration range to a mM solution of CDs (CA+Phen) in HEPES buffer solution, no change in the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra could be found. Only Fe2+ and Fe3+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs (CA+Phen) due to the presence of 1,10-phenanthroline on the surface of the CDs (CA+Phen). The capability of current CDs (CA+Phen) as a fluorescent probe to quantitatively detect Fe2+ and Fe3+ was also investigated.

UV–vis absorption spectra of CDs (CA+Phen) dissolved in HEPES buffer solution is analyze for Fe2+ and Fe3+ the UV spectrum shown in Fig. S2. The main absorption at around 271nm not have large change, but a new broad absorption peak from 300 to 500nm could be found and becomes more outspoken when in the addtion of 10μM Fe2+ and Fe3+. UV–vis spectrophotometric of 1,10-phenanthroline in the addtion of Fe2+ was carried out in HEPES buffer is depicted in Fig. S3. A new peak appears at approximately 500nm for 1,10-phenanthroline- Fe2+ complex could be found in Fig. S3. The red tail around 300–500nm for CDs (CA+Phen)- Fe2+/Fe3+ in Fig. S2 confirmed the presence of some residue of 1,10-phenanthroline on the surface of CDs (CA+Phen). In day light the spectral change results in a the clear color change from colorless to orange and golden yellow, when Fe2+ and Fe3+ is added to the HEPES buffer solution (Fig. S2).

The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (CA+Phen) are shown as a function of [Fe2+] in Fig. 5 a. Upon further increasing the Fe2+ concentration, a significant decrease of the fluorescence intensity and fluorescence quantum yield is observed with no other spectral changes (i.e. no spectral shifts or formation of new emission bands). The fluorescence emission spectra of CDs (CA+Phen) measured as a function of Fe3+ concentration are similar to those with Fe2+, but the sensitivity of CDs (CA+Phen) to Fe3+ (Fig. 5b) is lower than to Fe2+. When the concentration of Fe2+ reached 100μΜ, the fluorescence was quenched 92%. Whereas, when the concentration of Fe3+ reached 160μΜ, the fluorescence was quenched 93%.
The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs (CA+Phen) without and with high concentration of Fe2+ or Fe3+ was found to be 0.1, 0.008 (at [Fe2+]=100μM), and 0.007 (at [Fe3+]=160μM), respectively.

As shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence intensity decreased almost linearly with increasing concentration of Fe2+ (0–32μM) and Fe3+ (0–50μM) in HEPES buffer. The calibration curve for concentration of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in HEPES buffer was obtained as shown below, where F stands for the fluorescence peak intensity in HEPES buffer upon addition of Fe2+ or Fe3+.
F=2258−60.6×[Fe2+](r=0.996 [Fe2+]=0–32μM)
F=2760−31.5×[Fe3+](r=0.998 [Fe3+]=0–50μM)

The calibration equation serves as the quantitative basis for the detection of Fe2+ or Fe3+ in aqueous solution. The detection limit (DL) was estimated based on the following equation: DL=3×σ/k, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and k is the slope of the calibration curve. The DL of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was 20nM and 35nM in aqueous solution.

To investigate the selectivity of the binding event between CDs (CA+Phen) and Fe2+ and Fe3+. The selectivity of CDs (CA+Phen) was examined by using some interfering Metals such as (Li+, Na+, K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Al3+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Zn+2, Ag+, Hg2+ and NH4 +) under the identical conditions of Fe2+ and Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 7 . Only Fe2+ and Fe3+ induced a prominent intensity reduction. These results confirm that CDs (CA+Phen) shows an excellent selectivity towards Fe2+ and Fe3+. Therefore, this selective binding and the corresponding fluorescence quenching process can offer the CDs (CA+Phen) as a selective turn–off probe for Fe2+ and Fe3+.

Fluorescence quenching usually originate from static or dynamic quenching in which the fluorescent molecule react with the quencher. Static quenching involves the formation of the complex by combining the ground state fluorescence molecule with the quencher while in dynamic quenching the collision of excited fluorescence molecule with the quencher. To better explore the quenching mechanism, we also calculated the quenching equation according to the Stern–Volmer equation F 0/F =1+ ks[Q] from Fig. S5 and S6, where Ks is the association equilibrium constant, F0 is the initial fluorescence intensity without the quencher (Fe2+ or Fe3+), F is the fluorescence intensity after adding the quencher of concentration. As shown in Fig. S5. and S6 a good linear Stern–Volmer relationship is observed in CDs (CA+Phen)n for Fe2+ and Fe3+, Since the presence of Fe2+ or Fe3+ in the CDs (CA+Phen) aqueous solution does not change the fluorescence the lifetime of CDs (CA+Phen), we may conclude that the quenching process is a static mechanism.

To investigate the fluorescence dynamics of CDs (CA+Phen), fluorescence decay traces of CDs (CA+Phen) was recorded at 440nm by the single-photon timing method [41]. Fluorescence decay for CDs (CA+Phen) water revealed bi-exponential behavior. The fluorescence decay is fitted to the bi- exponential profile with the lifetime of ∼1.3ns (54.9%) and ∼4.4ns (45.1%) in water. Upon addition of 80μM Fe2+ or 160μM Fe3+ to aqueous solution of CDs (CA+Phen), the fluorescence decay time does not change (Fig. S4). The measured lifetime in the presence of Fe2+ (τ1 =1.3ns, 56.3% and τ2 =4.1ns, 43.7%) or Fe3+ (τ1 =1.4ns, 57.4% and τ2 =4.3ns, 42.6%) is the same as the lifetime of the CDs (CA+Phen) (τ1 =1.3ns, 54.9% and τ2 =4.4ns, 45.1%) in water. With the further increase of Fe2+ or Fe3+ concentration, the fluorescence decay time does not change either.

Quenching of fluorescence upon Fe2+ or Fe3+ binding arises from an ET (electron transfer) from the CDs (CA+Phen) fluorophore to the metal center. The decrease of the fluorescence intensity with increasing concentration of Fe2+ or Fe3+ means that the concentration of non-fluorescent form (complex) increases while that of the fluorescent form (CDs (CA+Phen)) decreases. This non-fluorescent complex species formed on addition of Fe2+ or Fe3+ doesn’t change the fluorescence emission maximum or the lifetime.

Carbon dots made from individual powder citric acid CDs (CA) doesn’t shown any selectivity for any metal ions (alkali, alkaline-earth, and transition metal ions) including Fe2+ or Fe3+ (Fig. S7). The carbon dots made from individual powder individual 1,10-phenanthroline CDs (Phen) shown some selectivity for Fe2+ or Fe3+, but other metal ions such as Zn 2+ and Co2+ have large interference (Fig. S8). As we mention before, carbon dots are versatile and tunable surface functionalities due to it have OH and COOH groups on its surface. Through surface functionalization, the residue 1,10-phenanthroline can easily modify on the surface on the citric acid and got the functionalized CDs (CA+Phen).

In the recent literature, some fluorescent CDS based chemosensors for Fe2+ or Fe3+ ions have been reported. In order to evaluate the evaluate the effectiveness of CDs (CA+Phen) as a potential probe for the determination of Fe2+ or Fe3+ performance of Fe2+ or Fe3+ as fluorescent probe, a comparison of the method with the published work related to fluorescence detection of Fe2+ or Fe3+ based on CDs is listed in Table 1 . It can be seen that the CDs (CA+Phen) shows an acceptable detection limit 20nM and 35nM which satisfactorily meets the limit of iron in drinking water set by the U.S (0.3mg/lit or ∼5.537μM), Environmental protection agency (EPA). The result indicated that the CDs (CA+Phen) could be potentially utilized as an excellent optical sensor for the quantitative analysis of Fe2+ or Fe3+ in aqueous solution.
So CDs (CA+Phen) have highly selectivity and sensitivity for Fe2+ or Fe3+. Schematic representation of formation of CDs (CA+Phen) and its binding with Fe2+ and Fe3+ are shown in Fig. 8 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of CDs (CA+Phen) in milk and cell (HK-2)
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practical feasibility of CDs (CA+Phen) for the detection of Fe in real sample of milk is carried out. In order to remove the proteins and fats from milk samples were first heated at 40°C then (20g/100mL) anhydrous Na2SO4 was added to the milk and then stir it well that whole Na2SO4 dissolve in milk then allow the milk for six hours to settle down at 25°C,the mix is filtered through fluted Whatman no. 4 papers. Discard the residue then for the complete removal of protein and fat from milk again added Na2SO4 and in the last 1% H2SO4 was added for the complete precipitation and then concentrate the filtrate to check out the Fe.

Determination of Fe in milk samples, the results obtained from CDs (CA+Phen) were compared to that obtained from ICP-AES (inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry) as shown in Table 2 . Good recoveries were obtained in the milk samples by this CDs (CA+Phen) with that obtained by ICP-AES. From this result it can be concluded that the CDs (CA+Phen) is sensitive, selective, simple and rapid for the determination of trace Fe in milk.

For practical applications, it is necessary that the molecular probe described here is able to detect Fe2+ or Fe3+ under biological conditions. Subsequent experiments probed the ability of CDs (CA+Phen) to track Fe2+ or Fe3+ levels in living cells using a confocal microscope. HK-2 cells were incubated with CDs (CA+Phen) and then further incubated with 80μM Fe2+ or 160μM Fe3+. Fig. 9 1a shows the bright field microscopy image of the HK-2 cells. The cells incubated with CDs (CA+Phen) show blue fluorescence when excited by ultraviolet light (Fig. 92b). From Figs. 9 2b and S12 it can be concluded that CDs (CA+Phen) efficiently accumulates inside the cytosol, but is excluded from the cell nucleus. In contrast, CDs (CA+Phen) stained cells exposed to Fe2+ or Fe3+ for 30min at 37°C display negligible intracellular fluorescence (Fig. 93b and 4b). These findings are consistent with the steady-state fluorescence experiment results discussed in previous section.

Moreover, cell cytotoxicity experiments of CDs (CA+Phen) were evaluated by using HK-2 cell lines through the MTS assay. As shown in Fig. S9, the CDs (CA+Phen) exhibited low cytotoxicity in cell, which may be due to the low toxicity of CDs (CA+Phen). The result confirmed that the probe CDs (CA+Phen) can be used to image intracellular Fe2+ or Fe3+ levels in living cells.

In short we have designed a highly photoluminescent CDs (CA+Phen) as shown in Scheme 1 (Fig. 8) prepared by a simple solid state method (without any solvents) from anhydrous citric acid and 1,10-phenanthroline shows strong blue fluorescence under excitation of a 365nm UV lamp with good dispersible in water. Fe2+ and Fe3+ can immediately quench the fluorescence of the CDs (CA+Phen) while other metal ions failed prompting the development of sensors for Fe2+ and Fe3+, with excellent selectivity by fluorescence based on CDs (CA+Phen)/Fe complex over other metals ions in aqueous solutions also shows good sensitivity with a low limit of detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ 20nM and 35nM. CDs (CA+Phen) can be applied to the detection of Fe2+ and Fe3+ in aqueous media. For only sensing of Fe3+ the Fe2+ can be oxidized from Fe2+ and Fe3+ by the use of oxidizing agent or can be airily oxidation. The application of quencher CDs (CA+Phen) for cell imaging is difficult but this CDs (CA+Phen) can be used for cell imaging. Additionally, this CDs (CA+Phen) has been successfully applied for the determination of Fe in real sample of milk. From this result it can be concluded that the CDs (CA+Phen) is sensitive, selective, simple and rapid for the determination of trace Fe in milk. Furthermore, no further chemical modification for CDs (CA+Phen) is required, which offers the advantages of simplicity and cost efficiency. We except that this strategy may offer a new approach for developing low-cost and sensitive sensors in biological and environmental applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21271094) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.81372733/H1619).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.06.126.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
